items.q: Is analysing existing products the same as copying them?
items.correct: No — analysis identifies strengths, weaknesses and gaps to inform your own original design; directly copying may infringe patents, trademarks or registered designs.
items.misconception: Designers are free to copy any product that is already on the market.
items.random_wrong: Product analysis is only legal on products more than 50 years old.
items.why: Students blur inspiration and imitation; intellectual property law is exactly the boundary between learning from a product and stealing its design.

items.q: What does ACCESS FM stand for?
items.correct: Aesthetics, Cost, Customer, Environment, Size, Safety, Function and Materials — a checklist of criteria for analysing a product.
items.misconception: ACCESS FM is a step-by-step method for assembling flat-pack products.
items.random_wrong: ACCESS FM is a British manufacturing safety standard.
items.why: Students remember the acronym but not the categories; without the eight criteria the analysis collapses into vague description.

items.q: Why disassemble a product during analysis rather than just look at it?
items.correct: Disassembly reveals internal components, material choices, fixings and assembly methods that are invisible from outside, showing how the product was engineered and made.
items.misconception: Disassembly is only ever done to repair products that have broken.
items.random_wrong: Disassembly is legally required before any product can be resold.
items.why: Students stop at surface features; taking products apart is how designers learn real manufacturing and assembly decisions, not just styling.

items.q: In ACCESS FM, what does the first 'A' stand for?
items.why: Correct answer: Aesthetics. “Assembly” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Why might a designer disassemble an existing product?
items.correct: To study its components, materials and assembly methods
items.misconception: To dispose of it in landfill
items.random_wrong: To make it lighter to post
items.why: It's To study its components, materials and assembly methods. “To dispose of it in landfill” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: When learning from competitors' products, designers must avoid what?
items.correct: Infringing patents and registered designs by copying
items.misconception: Looking at more than one product
items.why: Correct answer: Infringing patents and registered designs by copying. “Looking at more than one product” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What does an ergonomic evaluation of a product assess?
items.correct: How comfortably and easily people can use it
items.why: The answer examiners want is: How comfortably and easily people can use it. “How much profit it generates” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
